q: Sound is a sequence of pressure waves which propagates through compressible media.
Sound can propagate through media such as air or water.
During their propagation, waves cannot be reflected by the medium.
Waves can be refracted by the medium during propagation.
Sound waves can be attenuated by the medium through which they travel.
The speed of sound within a medium is independent of temperature.
A relationship between density and pressure determines the speed of sound within the medium.
The viscosity of the medium determines the rate at which sound is attenuated.
For media such as air or water, attenuation due to viscosity is significant.
Speed of a sound wave is affected by the density of the medium.
Temperature of the medium does not affect the speed of a sound wave.
The elasticity of the medium affects the speed of sound waves.
The speed of sound is the same for all different media.
In a solid medium, the speed of sound is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}$.
In the speed formula for solids, $Y$ represents the bulk modulus.
The symbol $\rho$ in speed formulas represents the density of the material.
In liquids, the velocity of sound is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$.
In the speed formula for liquids, $B$ represents Young's modulus.
In gases, the velocity of sound is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$.
In the gas velocity formula, $\gamma$ is the adiabatic constant of gas.
The symbol $P$ in the gas velocity formula represents the pressure in gas.
Longitudinal sound waves travel faster in gases than they do in liquids.
Sound waves travel faster in solids than they do in liquids.
Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are elasticity properties of the medium.
The SI units of Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are $N \cdot m^2$.
The SI units of Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are $N \cdot m^{-2}$.
The level of intensity of a sound is measured relative to a reference intensity value $I_0$.
The value of $I_0$ is taken as $10^{-12} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
The reference intensity $I_0$ corresponds to the threshold of hearing.
The threshold of hearing is also known as the maximum audible intensity.
The decibel ($dB$) is the unit used for measuring sound intensity.
The intensity level of sound $\beta$ in $dB$ is defined as $10 \log_{10} \frac{I}{I_0}$.
For every $10 dB$ added to the sound level, the loudness of the sound is increased by 10 times.
A $120 dB$ sound is 10 times louder than a $100 dB$ sound.
A $120 dB$ sound is 100 times louder than a $100 dB$ sound.
Sound intensity is a direct measure of the power reaching an eardrum.
Sound intensity is measured in $W \cdot m^{-2}$.
The intensity level for a jet plane taking off is $120 dB$.
The intensity in $W \cdot m^{-2}$ for a jet plane taking off is $10$.
Loud music has an intensity level of $100 dB$.
Busy street traffic corresponds to an intensity level of $70 dB$.
The intensity of busy street traffic is $10^{-5} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
People talking have an intensity level of $50 dB$.
The intensity level of a whisper is $20 dB$.
Whisper has an intensity of $10^{-10} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
Leaves falling correspond to an intensity level of $10 dB$.
The threshold of hearing corresponds to an intensity level of $0 dB$.
The intensity of the threshold of hearing is $10^{-12} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
For every $10 dB$ added, the loudness is increased by 100 times.
Sound is a sequence of pressure waves that propagates through incompressible media.
Echoes are produced where a sound wave is reflected from a large and hard surface.
An echo is a repetition of sound due to the reflection of sound.
The reflected sound can be heard separately from the original sound if the source is very far from the observer.
Reflection of sound obeys the laws of reflection.
Reflection of sound violates the laws of reflection.
To reduce the effect of echo in buildings, walls are covered with padding.
Floors are covered with rugs and carpets to reduce the effect of echo.
Padding, rugs, and carpets reflect the incident sound.
Padding, rugs, and carpets absorb the incident sound.
The amount of sound reflecting off wall surfaces is increased by padding.
Echo can be used to determine the distance $d$ between a source of sound and the reflecting surface.
The formula $v = \frac{2d}{t}$ is used to determine distance using echo.
In the echo formula, $v$ is the speed of sound and $t$ is the echo time.
Bats use echolocation to navigate and locate their prey.
Echolocation is the special ability of emitting sounds and interpreting the echoes.
A reverberation is a reflection of light or sound waves.
A reverberation is a wide reaching impact of an action.
Sound bouncing around in a large speaker is an example of reverberation.
Reverberation is the repetition of sound from a single reflection.
Padding and rugs increase the amount of sound reflecting off surfaces.
Audible means the ability to be heard.
Audibility means the capability of detecting sounds in a certain range of frequencies.
The human ear is capable of detecting sounds of all possible frequencies.
The range of frequencies a person can hear is known as the range of audibility.
For humans with normal hearing, the lower limit of audibility is $20 Hz$.
The upper limit of audibility for humans is $200,000 Hz$.
The upper limit of audibility for humans is about $20,000 Hz$.
The limits of human hearing are known as the limit of audibility.
A frequency generator can be used to determine the audible frequency range.
Portable sound frequency generators can create sound from $0 Hz$ up to $22 kHz$.
Limit of audibility is the same for all mammals.
The audible range of a bat extends up to $150,000 Hz$.
Dolphins have an audible range from $10,000 Hz$ to $120,000 Hz$.
A cat can detect frequencies as high as $65,000 Hz$.
A dog's audible range is $15 Hz$ to $50,000 Hz$.
The audible range for an elephant is $20 Hz$ to $12,000 Hz$.
The sonar works based on the principle of echoes.
Sonar stands for sound navigation and ranging.
Ships use sonar to detect the position of other vessels.
Sonar can be used to find the depth of the sea.
A transducer in sonar is used only to receive signals.
A transducer in sonar is also known as a transmitter.
The interval for the reflected signal in sonar is called the echo.
Infrasound waves are waves with frequencies higher than $20 Hz$.
Infrasound waves have frequencies less than $20 Hz$.
Infrasound waves have long wavelengths.
Infrasound waves have short wavelengths.
Infrasound waves are able to travel over long distances.
Elephants communicate over distances up to $4 km$ using infrasound.
Elephants use fundamental frequencies as low as $15 Hz$.
A rhinoceros uses frequencies down to $10 Hz$ to communicate.
Infrasound waves travel longer distances with more attenuation.
Infrasound waves travel longer distances with less attenuation.
Ultrasound can be used in medical imaging.
Frequencies used in imaging are typically in the range of $1$ to $15 MHz$.
Humans can hear infrasound.
Humans can hear ultrasound.
A rhinoceros can communicate over distances further than $4 km$.
The hearing range of a human is broader than that of a dolphin.
The voicing range of a dog is $450 Hz$ to $1,080 Hz$.
The voicing range of a bat is $7,000 Hz$ to $120,000 Hz$.
The hearing range for a cat is $60 Hz$ to $65,000 Hz$.
The voicing range of an elephant is $5 Hz$ to $12,000 Hz$.
The Doppler effect is a change in frequency of a wave related to an observer.
Doppler effect is caused by the relative motion between the source and observer.
The Doppler shift is a change in the wave's speed.
Christian Doppler was an Austrian physicist.
Christian Doppler described the phenomenon of Doppler effect in 1842.
Only the motion of the source and observer directly towards or away from each other matters in the reference frame.
Change of pitch heard when a vehicle sounding a horn approaches is an example of Doppler shift.
The general form of the Doppler equation is $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$.
In the Doppler equation, $f_s$ represents the frequency heard by the observer.
In the Doppler equation, $f_o$ represents the frequency heard by the observer.
The variable $v$ in the Doppler equation represents the speed of sound in the medium.
The variable $v_o$ in the Doppler equation represents the speed of the source.
The variable $v_s$ in the Doppler equation represents the speed of the source.
Appropriate signs must be taken into account when using the Doppler equation.
When source and observer approach each other, $f_o > f_s$.
When source and observer recede from each other, $f_o > f_s$.
When source and observer recede from each other, $f_o < f_s$.
$+v_o$ is used when the observer moves towards the source.
$-v_o$ is used when the observer moves towards the source.
$-v_o$ is used when the observer moves away from the source.
$-v_s$ is used when the source moves towards the observer.
$+v_s$ is used when the source moves towards the observer.
$+v_s$ is used when the source moves away from the observer.
When observer and source are stationary, $v_o = v_s = 0$.
Doppler effect is observed only in sound waves.
Doppler effect is observed in electromagnetic radiations.
Doppler effect can be observed in radio waves and microwaves.
Red shift is a manifestation of the Doppler effect in light from a star.
In red shift, wavelength shifts toward the red end of the spectrum.
Red shift corresponds to a higher frequency and shorter wavelength.
Red shift corresponds to a lower frequency and longer wavelength.
Red shift occurs when earth and a star move away from each other.
If a star approaches earth, spectral lines shift towards the blue end.
Radar stands for radio detection and ranging.
Radar is a form of echolocation that uses sound waves.
Radar uses electromagnetic waves.
The Doppler effect is used in radar to measure the velocity of detected objects.
Police radar and air traffic control radar use the Doppler effect.
Weather radar is an application of the Doppler effect.
Doppler ultrasound is used to examine blood flow.
Blood flow rates can be found by measuring the Doppler shift in ultrasound reflected by red blood cells.
Angiodynography is the technique measuring Doppler shift in ultrasound reflected by red blood cells.
Doppler ultrasound can help reveal blockage to blood flow.
Formation of plaque in arteries can be revealed by Doppler ultrasound.
Doppler ultrasound provides information on the heartbeat of a fetus during labor.
Echocardiography is the technique used to examine the heart.
Doppler ultrasound cannot be used to examine kidneys.
If source and observer move in the same direction with same velocity, there is no Doppler shift.
The pitch of the note is determined by its frequency.
The greater the frequency, the higher is the pitch.
Higher frequency results in a lower pitch of the note.
The loudness of the note depends upon the amplitude of the wave that produces it.
The greater the amplitude, the louder the note.
The greater the amplitude, the softer the note becomes.
Less energy is used to produce a larger amplitude.
More energy is used to produce a larger amplitude.
Quality of a sound is a characteristic that distinguishes sounds of different loudness.
Quality of a sound distinguishes two sounds of the same loudness and same pitch.
Timbre is another name for the quality of a sound.
Notes of the same pitch and loudness can be distinguished by their quality.
The timbre of a sound depends on its frequency.
The timbre of a sound depends on its waveform.
Waveform varies with the number of overtones or harmonics.
The notes produced by the violin are richer because it has fewer overtones.
Violin notes are richer because they have more overtones.
The intensity of the sound at a place is the energy transmitted per second per unit area.
The intensity of sound is the energy transmitted per minute per unit area.
Unit area for intensity measurement is held normally to the direction of sound.
The intensity of a wave is directly proportional to the amplitude of the wave.
The intensity of a wave is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude of the wave.
The relationship $I \propto A^2$ is correct for intensity and amplitude.
Loudness is a physical property that does not depend on the observer.
Loudness is a sensation and depends on the observer.
To the human ears, the loudness of the sound is objective.
To the human ears, the loudness of the sound is subjective.
Normally the greater the intensity, the greater the loudness of the sound is.
Low intensity sounds are always perceived as louder than high intensity sounds.
Angular frequency is a factor in the expression for intensity of sound.
Density of the medium is represented by $\rho$ in the intensity formula.
The variable $v$ in the intensity formula represents the velocity of the sound wave.
The intensity of a wave is inversely proportional to the square of the amplitude.
Two different musical instruments can produce notes of the same pitch.
Harmonics do not affect the waveform of a sound.
The intensity level is identical to the sensation of loudness.
Some people may find a volume of sound loud while others find it soft.
The timbre of a sound depends on the number of harmonics.
The energy transmitted per second per unit area defines the loudness.
Frequency determines how high a note is.
Frequency determines the amplitude of the wave.
Pitch and loudness are the same property of sound.
A larger amplitude is produced when more energy is used.
The quality of a sound distinguishes notes produced by different instruments.
The intensity formula $I = \frac{1}{2} \rho v \omega^2 A^2$ is correct.
Sound intensity is the energy transmitted per unit volume.
Intensity is proportional to the square of the angular frequency.
Timbre depends on the observer's subjective hearing.
Intensity is a sensation.
Frequency is the only factor determining the loudness of a sound.
